phrasalVerbs.definition: To talk about someone or something in a very positive way so that people are impressed with them.
examples: The media has been building up the candidate as a transformative leader for the nation.
The company's PR team worked tirelessly to build up the brand's reputation internationally.
Don't build up expectations too high—we need to manage stakeholder perceptions carefully.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To hide a problem or disagreement rather than finding a satisfactory solution to it.
examples: The reconciliation process merely papered over deep-rooted grievances within the organization.
You can't simply paper over fundamental design flaws and expect the system to function.
The diplomatic summit papered over tensions that would later escalate into open conflict.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To trick someone into believing something that is not true.
examples: Despite his scepticism, the experienced investigator was taken in by the elaborate cover story.
Consumers should be vigilant about being taken in by misleading advertising claims.
The fraudster had taken in numerous high-net-worth individuals before being apprehended.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To prove that something is true or correct.
examples: The independent audit bore out allegations of financial misconduct within the corporation.
Long-term studies have borne out the effectiveness of early intervention programmes.
Her intuition was borne out when the predicted market correction finally occurred.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To use information or your experience of something to help you do something.
examples: Successful leaders draw on diverse perspectives to make well-informed decisions.
The architect drew on traditional techniques while incorporating innovative materials.
In times of crisis, organisations must draw on their institutional knowledge and resilience.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To add more details or information to a plan or idea.
examples: The team will need to flesh out the proposal with concrete implementation timelines.
Your initial concept is compelling—now let's flesh out the supporting arguments.
The committee requested that we flesh out the risk assessment section more thoroughly.

fillInExercises.sentence: The marketing campaign has successfully ___ anticipation for the product launch.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Build up' means to promote something to create a positive impression.

fillInExercises.sentence: The agreement merely ___ over the underlying tensions between the two factions.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Paper over' means to hide problems rather than properly resolving them.

fillInExercises.sentence: Even experienced professionals can be ___ by sophisticated scam operations.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Take in' means to be deceived or tricked by someone.

fillInExercises.sentence: The empirical evidence has ___ out the theoretical framework proposed by the researchers.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Bear out' means to confirm or prove that something is accurate.

fillInExercises.sentence: The consultant ___ her extensive industry experience to develop innovative solutions.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Draw on' means to use knowledge or experience as a resource.

fillInExercises.sentence: The proposal is promising but needs to be ___ out with specific action items.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Flesh out' means to add more details to develop an idea fully.

fillInExercises.sentence: Subsequent investigations ___ the whistleblower's claims of corporate fraud.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Bear out' means to prove or substantiate that something is true.

fillInExercises.sentence: We need to ___ on our collective expertise to navigate this unprecedented challenge.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Draw on' means to utilise available resources or experience.

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: The board has been [building up|build up] this restructuring as a turning point for the company.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: I worry we're [papering over|paper over] some fundamental operational issues in the process.

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: You may be right. We shouldn't let ourselves be [taken in|take in] by optimistic projections.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: The quarterly results have [borne out|bear out] my concerns about declining market share.

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: Let's [draw on|draw on] the experience of successful turnaround specialists.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: Good idea. We can [flesh out|flesh out] a comprehensive recovery plan at the next meeting.

lines.speaker: Miguel
lines.text: Your paper [draws on|draw on] an impressive range of interdisciplinary sources.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: Thank you. I hope the empirical analysis [bears out|bear out] the theoretical claims.

lines.speaker: Miguel
lines.text: Some critics argue that you've [papered over|paper over] methodological limitations.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: I disagree, but I will [flesh out|flesh out] that section before publication.

lines.speaker: Miguel
lines.text: Don't let yourself be [taken in|take in] by peer pressure to dilute your conclusions.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: Absolutely not. I've spent years [building up|build up] the evidence base for this work.

lines.speaker: Miguel
lines.text: Your persistence will be [borne out|bear out] in the long-term impact of this research.
